articles.title: UK House Prices Go Down Because of War Worries
articles.summary: In March, the average price of houses in the UK fell by 0.5%, according to Halifax, a big mortgage lender. The average house now costs £299,677. This drop happened because of the war in Iran, which made people worried. The war increased energy costs, leading to higher inflation and mortgage rates. Mortgage rates went up, and many cheap deals disappeared. Amanda Bryden from Halifax said the uncertainty about the war reduced confidence in the housing market. Oil prices also rose due to the war, but they fell a bit after ceasefire plans. However, mortgage rates did not improve. Interest rates on new mortgages have risen sharply, with the average two-year deal now at 5.90%. The future of house prices depends on how long these pressures last and their effect on the economy and jobs.

articles.title: UK Leader Visits Middle East After Peace Deal
articles.summary: Sir Keir Starmer, the UK prime minister, is traveling to the Middle East to talk with leaders after the US and Iran agreed to stop fighting. This ceasefire means they will not attack each other for now, which brings relief to the region. Sir Keir wants to help make this peace last and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, an important waterway for ships. He will also meet UK soldiers there. Other UK politicians have different opinions: some say Iran must make good choices, while others worry about trusting the US. The UK recently held meetings with many countries to plan for security in the area after the fighting stops.

articles.title: Doctors on Strike: How Hospitals Are Doing
articles.summary: In England, doctors are on strike for six days to ask for more pay. The head of the NHS, Sir Jim Mackey, says hospitals are handling it well so far. Doctors who are still learning, called resident doctors, are not working. This is their 15th strike because they want better pay. To help, senior doctors are working extra in emergencies. Some planned surgeries had to be canceled, but most are still happening. The strike started after Easter, making it hard for hospitals. The doctors' group says the government did not offer a good deal. The government says they gave doctors a good pay raise and other help. Everyone hopes the strike ends soon.

articles.title: Trump and Iran Make a Deal to Stop Fighting
articles.summary: President Donald Trump and Iran have made a deal to stop fighting for two weeks. Trump had set a deadline to attack Iran if no deal was made, but just before that, they agreed to a ceasefire. This means both sides will not fight and will talk to try to make peace forever. Iran will also let ships pass through the Strait of Hormuz, which is important for oil. The deal helped Trump avoid a hard choice between starting a big war or looking weak. Oil prices went down and stock markets went up because people are hopeful the fighting might end. But some people, like Democrats, criticized Trump's strong words about Iran. The deal is temporary, so they need to work hard in the next two weeks to make it last.

articles.title: Iran's Reaction to a Peace Agreement
articles.summary: President Trump threatened Iran but later agreed to a two-week ceasefire. In Iran, people are reacting to this news. Special correspondent Reza Sayah in Tehran explains that Iran's government has accepted the ceasefire proposal. He says Iran might call this a victory because they are still standing despite challenges. State media headlines show Iran feeling strong and making Trump retreat. This story helps us understand how countries talk and make peace instead of fighting. It shows that words and actions can change quickly in international relations, and staying calm can be important.

articles.title: Iran and the U.S. Talk About Peace
articles.summary: This article talks about Iran and the United States trying to make peace. They agreed to stop fighting for two weeks. Experts Alan Eyre and Miad Maleki share their thoughts. Alan was part of a team that made a deal with Iran before, and Miad knows about Iran from growing up there and working in the U.S. government. They discuss what might happen next. Alan wonders if Iran will give up control of the Strait of Hormuz, which is important for oil ships. The ceasefire is a small step, but it could help avoid more problems. Both experts think it's good to talk, but they are not sure if peace will last. The article helps us understand why countries negotiate and what makes it hard.
